1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. 12	# The Riemann–Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)
13	
14	This file is the **RR.2** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
15	headline `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically
16	irreducible genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
17	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
18	
19	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
20	through:
21	
22	- `RR.1` (`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` / `WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil
23	  divisor group `Div(C)` and the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
24	- **`RR.2` (this file, `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`)**: the Riemann–Roch
25	  dimension formula `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0` (with `deg D ≥ 0`),
26	  via the auxiliary Euler-characteristic identity
27	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`.
28	- `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, future): the invertible sheaf
29	  `𝒪_C(D)`, the linear-equivalence isomorphism `𝒪_C(D) ≅ 𝒪_C(D')` for
30	  `D ∼ D'`, and the `H¹`-vanishing input
31	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(D)) = 0` for `deg D ≥ 0` on a genus-`0` curve.
32	- `RR.4` (`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`, future): the "two-section
33	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
34

101	/-! ## §1. The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve -/
102	
103	/-- **Euler characteristic of a `ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf on a smooth proper
104	curve `C / k̄`.**
105	
106	On a curve (one-dimensional scheme), Grothendieck vanishing
107	(Hartshorne III.2.7) gives `H^i(C, 𝓕) = 0` for `i ≥ 2`, so the classical
108	alternating sum
109	`χ(𝓕) = Σ_{i ≥ 0} (-1)^i dim_{k̄} H^i(C, 𝓕)` collapses to the two-term
110	expression
111	`χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)`.
112	
113	This is the definition we ship. Coherence of `𝓕` on the proper `k̄`-scheme
114	`C` guarantees that both `H⁰` and `H¹` are finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector
115	spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem, the same
116	finiteness backing `AlgebraicGeometry.genus`), so the two `Module.finrank`s
117	are honest natural numbers and the difference is a well-defined integer.
118	
119	The `Module k̄`-valued cohomology pipeline is the project's
120	`Scheme.HModule` (iter-009), the same wrapper used by
121	`AlgebraicGeometry.genus`.
122	
123	Blueprint reference: `def:eulerChar_curve`
124	(Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295, displayed inside the proof of Theorem 1.3). -/
125	noncomputable def Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
126	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
127	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
128	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
129	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
130	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
131	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : ℤ :=
132	  (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 0) : ℤ)
133	    - (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 1) : ℤ)

156	/-! ## §3. The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor -/
157	
158	/-- **The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper curve
159	`C / k̄`.**
160	
161	By definition,
162	`ℓ(D) := dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D)) ∈ ℕ`,
163	the `k̄`-dimension of the space of global sections of the invertible sheaf
164	`𝒪_C(D)` associated to `D`. Finiteness of `ℓ(D)` is a consequence of the
165	coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem on a proper `k̄`-scheme
166	(Hartshorne II.5.19 / III.5.2), the same input that backs the finiteness
167	of `genus C`.
168	
169	The classical *complete linear system* `|D|` has `k̄`-projective dimension
170	`ℓ(D) − 1`; the chapter never uses this projective interpretation —
171	`ℓ(D)` is the only quantity consumed by the Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain.
172	
173	Blueprint reference: `def:l_invariant` (Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295,
174	"We denote `dim_k H⁰(X, 𝒪(D))` by `l(D)`"). -/
175	noncomputable def l (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) : ℕ :=
176	  Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar (sheafOf (C := C) D) 0)
177	
178	end Scheme.WeilDivisor

683	/-- **Euler-characteristic identity for `𝒪_C(D)` on a smooth proper curve
684	of genus `g`.**
685	
686	For every Weil divisor `D ∈ Div(C)`,
687	`χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g(C)`.
688	
689	The proof is Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3's reduction: induction on the
690	free-abelian-group structure of `Div(C)` on closed points, base case `D =
691	0` giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` (the `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` is the Hartshorne
692	I.3.4 input via the project's `H⁰`-bridge), and inductive step via
693	additivity of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence
694	`0 → 𝒪_C(D) → 𝒪_C(D + [P]) → k(P) → 0`.

697	(Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3, p. 295).

713	  -- Unfold `WeilDivisor` to expose the underlying `Finsupp` structure so

723	    rw [Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add, hD']

738	/-- **Riemann–Roch in genus zero (Hartshorne IV.1 Example 1.3.5).**
739	
740	Let `C` be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over the
741	algebraically closed field `k̄` with `g(C) = 0`, and let `D ∈ Div(C)` with
742	`deg D ≥ 0`. Then
743	`ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1`.
744	
745	The proof specialises the χ-identity
746	`eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus` to `g = 0`,
747	unfolds `χ` via `def:eulerChar_curve`, and absorbs the `H¹`-vanishing
748	hypothesis (named premise `_hH1`: `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(D)) = 0`, which is the
749	`H¹`-vanishing of a non-negative-degree invertible sheaf on a
750	genus-`0` curve, to be discharged by `RR.3` once `𝒪_C(D)` has a body and
751	the cohomology of `𝒪_{ℙ¹}(d)` is computed).
752	
753	Blueprint reference: `thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero` (Hartshorne IV.1
754	Example 1.3.5, p. 297). -/

758	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
759	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
760	    (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) (_hg : AlgebraicGeometry.genus C = 0)
761	    (_hdeg : (0 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D)
762	    (_hH1 : Module.finrank kbar
763	      (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D) 1) = 0) :

10	/-!
11	# Vanishing of `H¹` for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H¹)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.2.H¹** project-side build of the closed-point
14	skyscraper `H¹`-vanishing identity
15	
16	  `dim_{k̄} H¹(C, skyscraperSheaf P (ModuleCat.of k̄ k̄)) = 0`
17	
18	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`. The file is
19	the iter-191 Lane H **file-skeleton**: each of the eight pinned
20	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching
21	the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/RiemannRoch_H1Vanishing.tex`),
22	with `sorry` bodies; the iter-192+ closure follows the classical
23	Hartshorne III.2.5 flasque-vanishing argument.
24	
25	The chapter strategy (Hartshorne III §2): an injective resolution of a
26	flasque sheaf yields a flasque quotient at each stage; the
27	global-sections functor is exact on a flasque-to-flasque-to-flasque
28	short exact sequence; hence the right-derived global-sections functor
29	vanishes on a flasque input in positive degree. We specialise the
30	abstract flasque-vanishing to the closed-point skyscraper sheaf, which
31	is flasque because it is the pushforward, along the closed embedding of
32	a one-point subspace, of the constant sheaf on an irreducible base.
33	
34	## Eight pinned declarations
35	
36	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque` — predicate on
37	   `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`.
38	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward` — pushforward of a
39	   flasque sheaf is flasque (Hartshorne II.1, Ex. 1.16(d)).
40	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible` —
41	   constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque (Hartshorne
42	   II.1, Ex. 1.16(a)).
43	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero` — flasque sheaves
44	   have zero `HModule` in positive degree (Hartshorne III.2.5).
45	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const` —
46	   skyscraper sheaf is the pushforward of a constant sheaf along the
47	   closed embedding of the closure of the support point.
48	6. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible` — the
49	   closure of the support point of a `PrimeDivisor` is irreducible.
50	7. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` —
51	   closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque.
52	8. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero` — the
53	   `RR.2.H¹` headline (`dim_{k̄} H¹(C, k(P)) = 0`), obtained by composing
54	   declarations 4 and 7.

56	The eighth declaration also lives as a `private` typed-`sorry` helper at

80	/-! ## §1. Flasque sheaves of `kbar`-modules -/
81	
82	/-- **Flasque sheaf of `kbar`-modules on a topological space**
83	(Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16; III.2, paragraph preceding Lemma 2.4).
84	
85	A sheaf `F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`
86	is **flasque** when, for every inclusion of opens `V ≤ U` in `X`, the
87	restriction map
88	`F.val.obj (op U) → F.val.obj (op V)` is surjective as a map of
89	`kbar`-modules.
90	
91	The predicate is read off the underlying presheaf of `kbar`-modules and
92	applies uniformly to the project's `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured cohomology
93	pipeline (it does not depend on any topological-space hypothesis on `X`,
94	so the curve specialisation is by instance synthesis on
95	`C.left.toTopCat`).
96	
97	Blueprint reference: `def:isFlasque_sheaf`. -/
98	def Scheme.IsFlasque
99	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
100	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : Prop :=
101	  ∀ ⦃U V : TopologicalSpace.Opens X⦄ (h : V ≤ U),
102	    Function.Surjective ((F.val.map (homOfLE h).op).hom)
103	
104	/-- **Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque** (Hartshorne II.1,
105	Exercise 1.16(d)).
106	
107	For a continuous map of topological spaces `f : X ⟶ Y` and a flasque
108	sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on `X`, the pushforward sheaf `f _* F` is
109	flasque on `Y`. The reason is purely formal: the restriction map of
110	`f _* F` along `V ≤ U` in `Y` is, by definition of pushforward, the
111	restriction map of `F` along `f ⁻¹ V ≤ f ⁻¹ U` in `X`, which is
112	surjective by hypothesis on `F`.
113	
114	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via the unfolding
115	`pushforward.map (homOfLE h).op = F.val.map ((Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)).op`
116	(by `rfl`), so flasqueness of `F` along `(Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)` gives
117	the conclusion. Blueprint reference: `lem:isFlasque_pushforward`. -/
118	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward
119	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X Y : TopCat.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y)
120	    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
121	    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque F) :
122	    Scheme.IsFlasque
123	      ((TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) f).obj F) := by
124	  intro U V h
125	  exact hF ((Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)).le
126	
127	/-- **Constant sheaf on an irreducible topological space is flasque**
128	(Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16(a)).
129	
130	For an irreducible topological space `X`, every nonempty open is
131	connected and dense; the constant presheaf `U ↦ A` already satisfies the
132	sheaf condition, and its restriction maps are the identity on `A` (for
133	the nonempty branches) and factor through `0` (for the empty branch).
134	
135	**iter-191 Lane H file-skeleton** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry; closure
136	is iter-192+. Blueprint reference:
137	`lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible`. -/
138	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible
139	    (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
140	    [IrreducibleSpace X] (A : ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :
141	    Scheme.IsFlasque
142	      ((constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
143	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A) := by
144	  sorry
145	
146	/-- **Injective sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology** (axiom-clean
147	helper, Hartshorne III §1).
148	
149	For a topological space `X` and an injective object `I` of the
150	`kbar`-module sheaf category, the `kbar`-flavoured derived
151	global-sections cohomology `HModule kbar I i` is the zero `kbar`-module
152	for every `i ≥ 1`. This is immediate from Mathlib's
153	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT` framework: `Injective I` gives
154	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT I 1` (Mathlib
155	`instHasInjectiveDimensionLTOfNatNatOfInjective`), and
156	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` yields
157	`Subsingleton (Abelian.Ext Y I i)` for `i ≥ 1` and any `Y`; specialising
158	`Y` to the constant sheaf at `kbar` and applying
159	`Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton` closes the goal.
160	
161	**iter-192 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed axiom-clean. Used as the
162	`Ext^i(_, I) = 0` input inside the Hartshorne III.2.5 long-exact-sequence
163	chase for `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` below. -/
164	theorem Scheme.HModule_injective_finrank_eq_zero
165	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
166	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
167	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
168	    {I : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
169	    [Injective I] (i : ℕ) (hi : 1 ≤ i) :
170	    Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar I i) = 0 := by
171	  have hsub : Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar I i) :=
172	    HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton I 1 i hi _
173	  exact Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton
174	
175	/-- **The canonical injective-embedding short exact sequence**
176	`0 → F → Injective.under F → cokernel(Injective.ι F) → 0` of sheaves of
177	`kbar`-modules (axiom-clean helper). The embedding `Injective.ι F` is
178	mono by `Injective.ι_mono`, the cokernel projection is epi by
179	construction, and the middle row is exact at `Injective.under F` by
180	`ShortComplex.exact_cokernel`. -/
181	noncomputable def Scheme.injectiveSES
182	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
183	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
184	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
185	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :
186	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
187	      (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :=
188	  ShortComplex.mk (Injective.ι F) (Limits.cokernel.π (Injective.ι F))
189	    (Limits.cokernel.condition (Injective.ι F))
190	
191	/-- The injective-embedding short complex is short exact. -/
192	theorem Scheme.injectiveSES_shortExact
193	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
194	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
195	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
196	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :
197	    (Scheme.injectiveSES F).ShortExact :=
198	  { exact := ShortComplex.exact_cokernel (Injective.ι F)
199	    mono_f := Injective.ι_mono F
200	    epi_g := Limits.coequalizer.π_epi }
201	
202	/-- **Generic LES vanishing lemma**: in an abelian category with enough Ext,
203	if `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` is short exact with `S.X₂` injective and the
204	post-composition `Hom(X, S.X₂) → Hom(X, S.X₃)` is surjective, then
205	`Ext X S.X₁ 1` is the zero `Subsingleton`.
206	
207	This is the structural skeleton of the Hartshorne III.2.5 argument
208	specialised at degree 1: the long exact sequence
209	`Ext X S.X₂ 0 → Ext X S.X₃ 0 → Ext X S.X₁ 1 → Ext X S.X₂ 1`
210	collapses because the rightmost term vanishes (injectivity of `S.X₂`)
211	and the leftmost map is surjective by the `Hom`-level hypothesis. The
212	proof composes:
213	- `HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` (injective ⇒ `Ext X S.X₂ 1 = 0`).
214	- `Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁` (any `x₁ ∈ Ext X S.X₁ 1` lifts
215	  to some `x₃ ∈ Ext X S.X₃ 0` via the connecting morphism).
216	- `Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀` (identifies `Ext X S.X₃ 0` with `Hom(X, S.X₃)`).
217	- `comp_extClass_assoc` (the LES "complex" identity used to discharge
218	  `(Ext.mk₀ S.g).comp extClass = 0`).
219	
220	**iter-192 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed axiom-clean. Used to peel
221	off the `i = 1` case of `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` once the

12	\section{Setup and motivation}

14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]

26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]

85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
99	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
106	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
120	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
131	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
132	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}

157	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer

183	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
184	
185	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
186	
187	The proof of REF below
188	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
189	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
190	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
191	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
192	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
193	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
194	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
195	of sheaves; the third
196	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
197	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
198	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
199	independently of the parent inductive argument.

201	\begin{lemma}\leanok
202	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
203	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
205	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
206	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
207	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
208	  % short exact sequence) (read from
209	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
211	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
212	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
214	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
215	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
216	  \(\bar k\), and let
217	  \[
218	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
219	  \]
220	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
221	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
222	  \[
223	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
224	  \]
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
229	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
230	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
231	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
232	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
233	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
234	  resulting nine-term sequence
235	  \[
236	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
237	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
238	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
239	  \]
240	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
241	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
242	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
243	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
244	  \[
245	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
246	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
247	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
248	  \]
249	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
250	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
251	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
252	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
253	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
254	  \[
255	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
256	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
257	    \;=\; 0,
258	  \]
259	  which rearranges, using the definition
260	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
261	  (REF), to
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
263	
264	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
265	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
266	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
267	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
268	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
269	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge

274	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`

276	\end{proof}

278	\begin{lemma}\leanok
279	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
280	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
282	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
283	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
284	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
285	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
286	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
287	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
288	  Then
289	  \[
290	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
291	  \]
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
296	  \leanok
297	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
298	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
299	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
300	  \[
301	    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
302	  \]
303	  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
304	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
305	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
306	  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
307	  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
308	  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
309	  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
310	  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
311	  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
312	  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
313	  degrees gives
314	  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
315	  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
316	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
317	  (REF) closes the goal.
318	\end{proof}

480	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
481	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
482	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
483	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
484	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
485	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
486	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
487	  % is the Euler characteristic

490	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
491	  \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then
492	  \[

494	  \]

497	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case \(D = 0\). Then our
498	% formula says
499	% \(\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g\),
500	% because \(H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k\) for any projective variety
501	% (I, 3.4), and \(\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g\) by (1.1).
502	% Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point. We will show
503	% that the formula is true for \(D\) if and only if it is true for
504	% \(D + P\). Since any divisor can be reached from \(0\) in a finite number
505	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
506	% the result holds for all \(D\).
507	% We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its structure sheaf is
508	% the skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\), which we denote by
509	% \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\) by (II, 6.18).
510	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
511	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
512	% Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
513	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
514	% (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
515	% does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\)). Now the Euler characteristic is
516	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
517	% = 1$, so we have
518	% \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\).
519	% On the other hand, \(\deg D + P = \deg D + 1\), so our formula is true
520	% for \(D\) if and only if it is true for \(D + P\), as required."

522	  \leanok
523	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:l_invariant,
524	    lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
525	    lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
526	
527	  The proof is by induction on the structure of \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as the free
528	  abelian group on closed points (REF,
529	  REF). Both sides of the asserted
530	  equation are additive in \(D\) (the right-hand side by linearity of
531	  \(\deg\), REF; the left-hand side by the
532	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
533	  for \(D = 0\) and to show that it transports across the elementary
534	  modification \(D \mapsto D + [P]\) at any closed point \(P \in C\).
535	
536	  \emph{Base case \(D = 0\).} The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) is the
537	  structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_C\), and the formula
538	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\) reads
539	  \[
540	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
541	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
542	    \;=\; 1 - g.
543	  \]
544	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
545	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g\) (REF). The
546	  first term is \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\): \(C\) is a
547	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
548	  closed field \(\bar k\), so its connected components agree with its
549	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
550	  \cong \bar k\( as \)\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
551	  statement that ``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\) for any projective
552	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
553	  the project's \(H^0\)-bridge in
554	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
555	  two values gives \(1 - g = 1 - g\), as required.

557	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)''
558	  step).} Let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
559	  argument: view \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(C\); its structure
560	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\) supported at \(P\),
561	  and its sheaf of ideals is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\)
562	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out \(P\) in \(C\)). Hence
563	  the short exact sequence
564	  \[
565	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
566	  \]
567	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
568	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) (a locally free
569	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module of rank \(1\), so tensoring preserves exactness
570	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged) yields
571	  \[
572	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
573	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
574	    \to k(P) \to 0.
575	  \]
576	  (Strictly: tensoring \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\) with
577	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) produces a sheaf isomorphic — but not
578	  syntactically equal — to \(\mathcal O_C(D)\), and the skyscraper
579	  factor \(k(P)\) is preserved up to isomorphism because
580	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank \(1\). We transport
581	  along this isomorphism via iso-invariance of \(\chi\),
582	  REF.)
583	  Additivity of \(\chi\) on the short exact sequence
584	  (REF, the project-side
585	  factoring of the ``Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
586	  exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1)'' input of Hartshorne~IV.1.3) gives
587	  \[
588	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
589	    \;=\;
590	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
591	  \]
592	  The skyscraper-sheaf input
593	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) is the standalone substrate lemma
594	  REF (factored from the
595	  same Hartshorne~IV.1.3 line); its proof splits into the
596	  H\(^0\) identification \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and the
597	  H\(^1\) vanishing \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\), pursued
598	  independently by the prover (see the lemma's two-paragraph proof
599	  body). Therefore
600	  \[
601	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
602	  \]
603	  On the other hand, \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
604	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), so by
605	  REF
606	  \[
607	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
608	  \]
609	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
610	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) holds for \(D\) if and only
611	  if it holds for \(D + [P]\). Since the closed-point divisors \([P]\)
612	  generate \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
613	  from \(D = 0\) to every \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) by induction on the (signed)
614	  number of generators in the expression of \(D\).

618	  lemmas, each pinned to its own Lean declaration in

624	  REF (\(\chi\) on

627	  identity REF

629	  the project's existing \(H^0\)-bridge and an H\(^1\) half gated on

641	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
642	
643	\begin{theorem}
644	
645	
646	\leanok
647	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
648	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
649	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
650	  
651	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-\(0\) specialisation
652	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
653	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
654	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
655	  % if it is birational to \(\mathbf P^1\) (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
656	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
657	  % \(X\) is rational if and only if \(X \cong \mathbf P^1\) (I, 6.12). Now
658	  % using (1.3) we can show that \(X\) is rational if and only if \(g = 0\).
659	  % We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so
660	  % conversely suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two
661	  % distinct points of \(X\) and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
662	  % \(D = P - Q\). Since \(\deg K - D = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have
663	  % \(l(K - D) = 0\), and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of
664	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words $P \sim
665	  % Q\(. But this implies \)X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
666	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
667	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
668	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with
669	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). Then
670	  \[
671	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
672	  \]
673	\end{theorem}
674	
675	\begin{proof}
676	  \leanok
677	  
678	  Specialising
679	  REF to the genus-\(0\)
680	  hypothesis \(g = 0\) yields
681	  \[
682	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
683	  \]
684	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF and the definition
685	  \(\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\)
686	  (REF), this rewrites as
687	  \[
688	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
689	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
690	  \]
691	  Under the standing genus-\(0\) hypothesis and the assumption
692	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\), the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
693	  \[
694	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
695	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
696	  \]
697	  Substituting yields \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\), as required.
698	
699	  \emph{The \(H^1\)-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
700	  irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), the vanishing
701	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) is the Hartshorne

703	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\); equivalently, for a curve where every
704	  degree-\(0\)-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
705	  sections after a single point-twist, \(H^1\) of the twist must vanish
706	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
707	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this \(H^1\)
708	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter

711	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
712	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
713	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
714	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
715	  \(H^1\)-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
716	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
717	  follow-up signature simplification.
718	
719	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
720	  this theorem is the genus-\(0\) classification
721	  REF of
722	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\)

# AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean

## Iter-192 summary

**Result: HARD BAR MET (file: 2 → 1 sorry).**

- Added `import AlgebraicJacobian.RiemannRoch.H1Vanishing`.
- Removed the local `private theorem Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero`
  (it was a typed-sorry shadow of the public declaration in `H1Vanishing.lean`).
  Replaced with a docstring-only comment block explaining the new sourcing.
- The downstream consumer `Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf`
  references `Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero C P`; after the
  deletion the name resolves to the imported public version (iter-191
  Lane H), which closes via composition of `HModule_flasque_eq_zero`
  (still typed-sorry in H1Vanishing, Hartshorne III.2.5) and the
  axiom-clean `skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque`. So the H1 chain consumption
  is complete — `eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf` now traces all
  the way to the H1Vanishing carrier instead of stopping at a local sorry.

Build verifies cleanly:
- `lake build AlgebraicJacobian.RiemannRoch.RRFormula` → success.
- 1 remaining sorry warning: line 335 (`eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add`).
- Axiom probe on key declarations: `propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound,
  sorryAx`. The transitive `sorryAx` is inherited from
  `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` (H1Vanishing.lean) and the remaining local
  `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` — both are off-critical-path
  documented blockers.

## H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero (former line 468)

### Attempt 1 (iter-192)
- **Approach:** Add `import AlgebraicJacobian.RiemannRoch.H1Vanishing` and
  replace the body of the local `private` shadow theorem with a
  delegation `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero C P`.
- **Result:** FAILED on first build — error
  ``a non-private declaration `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero`
   has already been declared``. The `private` modifier in Lean 4 still
  registers the full namespaced name in the environment; importing the
  public version collides regardless of file-local visibility.

### Attempt 2 (iter-192)
- **Approach:** Delete the local `private theorem` entirely and replace
  with a docstring-only `/-! ... -/` block explaining the sourcing
  change. The downstream consumer call inside
  `eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf` resolves to the public version
  automatically.
- **Result:** RESOLVED. `lake build` succeeds with the H1 sorry
  removed; only the `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` sorry remains.
- **Key insight:** `private` in Lean 4 controls *visibility* (callability
  from outside the file), not *namespace registration*. The fully
  qualified name still goes into the environment, so a `private`
  declaration cannot share a name with an `import`ed declaration even
  though it cannot be referenced from outside.

## eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add (line 323; body at line 335)

### Attempt 1 (iter-192) — push-beyond evaluation
- **Approach:** Assess feasibility of closing within iter-192 budget.
- **Result:** DEFERRED. The PUSH-BEYOND condition in the directive was
  "if [the other sorry] is a downstream consumer of the same chain".
  `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` is **not** a consumer of the H1
  skyscraper chain — it is a general χ-additivity-on-SES claim that
  needs different infrastructure:
  1. The project-side `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised
     to `Scheme.HModule kbar` (Mathlib provides
     `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence_exact` but the specialisation has
     not been done in the project).
  2. Grothendieck vanishing `HModule kbar F i = 0` for `i ≥ 2` on a
     1-dimensional scheme — at the project `Abelian.Ext (constSheaf
     kbar, -)` level (Mathlib has no schemes/curve-cohomology
     dimension lemma at this level).
  3. The 6-term alternating rank identity on `kbar`-finite-dimensional
     vector spaces (provable from `LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker`
     plus iteration; ~30-60 LOC).
- **Dead end:** Closing the body requires (1)+(2) which together are
  the ~150-300 LOC sub-build flagged as off-critical-path in the
  blueprint's `% NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness
  machinery.` annotation (chapters/RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex L264-275).
  The piece (3) alone does not close the sorry.
- **Next step (iter-193+):** Either factor piece (3) into a standalone
  helper for partial progress, or escalate via `mathlib-build` mode for
  the project-side LES carrier + Grothendieck vanishing pieces.

## Blueprint markers

- `lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero` (RRFormula.tex L342) was
  formerly `\leanok` on the type signature only because the local copy
  carried a sorry. After this iter, the public version in
  H1Vanishing.lean is the authoritative carrier; sync_leanok should
  recompute `\leanok` based on the H1Vanishing decl's axiom status
  (`sorryAx` still inherited from `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` body).
- All other declarations in RRFormula.lean retain their existing
  `\leanok` status (no signature changes).

## Lemmas discovered

- `CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence_exact` (Mathlib) —
  exact 6-term LES carrier for an SES in any HasExt abelian category.
- `Mathlib.ModuleCat.free_shortExact_finrank_add` — additivity of
  finrank on a 3-term SES of finite free modules.
- `LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker` — the basic rank-nullity
  needed to iterate into a 6-term alternating-rank identity.

These are the building blocks any iter-193+ closure attempt should
consume.

## Push-beyond verdict

Genuinely blocked. Both sorries that remain in the file at iter-191
exit are gated on different substrate pieces:
- H1 sorry: chain on H1Vanishing.lean's `HModule_flasque_eq_zero`
  (Hartshorne III.2.5, iter-192+ Lane H critical-path target).
- shortExact_add sorry: project-side LES specialisation + Grothendieck
  H²-vanishing at the Ext-cohomology level.

This iter cleared the H1 sorry by importing the iter-191 substrate;
the shortExact_add sorry requires a different lane.
